nativeRange.habitat: Rocky substrates and coral reef flats in the photic zone; tends to form turf-like clumps on hard surfaces such as live rock and coral skeleton.

avoidWith: herbivorous fish that may sample it

imageCandidates.notes: Pillow Moss product listing image — tufted green clump on plug.
Maiden's Hair listing image — same genus, alternate SKU.

summary: Pillow Moss (Chlorodesmis sp.) is a tufted green macroalgae that anchors to live rock and hard surfaces in reef aquariums, forming stiff, pillowy or hair-like clumps. It produces a natural toxin that tends to deter herbivores but may stress corals placed in direct contact.
careNotes:
Pillow Moss forms dense, turf-like tufts of stiff green filaments on live rock and hard substrate in reef aquariums. It tends to grow slowly and is generally considered more demanding than most commonly kept macroalgae, with the genus rated at advanced to expert difficulty across multiple hobby references, citing sensitivity to elevated nitrates, dissolved copper, and declining water quality. High-intensity lighting is commonly recommended for successful growth, along with moderate to strong water flow to prevent detritus from accumulating on the thallus (the body of the alga). Trace element and iron supplementation may benefit long-term growth.

The genus produces chlorodesmin, a toxic diterpene (a class of natural chemical compound) that has been reported to deter most herbivorous fish from grazing on it. However, this same toxin may stress or inhibit corals placed in direct contact with the algae, so it is generally advised to maintain a gap between Chlorodesmis clumps and adjacent coral colonies. No dissolved copper should be present in the system, as the genus is sensitive to it.

This species complex includes the 'Pillow Moss' tufted form and the 'Maiden's Hair' finer-textured form, both sold under the genus label Chlorodesmis sp. The named species Chlorodesmis fastigiata, commonly called Turtle Weed, is treated as a separate entry in this database [sw-algae-101]. Pillow Moss is currently considered a specialty item in the aquarium trade and is sold primarily through specialty vendors rather than general aquarium retailers.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is most reliably achieved by fragmentation — breaking off a portion of an established clump and wedging or attaching it to a new piece of rock or hard surface. Sporulation has been documented in natural populations but is not a commonly used method in captive culture.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Product page confirms genus, growth form (stiff pillowy tufts on hard substrate), and slow growth. No numerical care parameters provided — Mosaic is the sole hobby source for this SKU.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros — Maiden's Hair listing
additional.notes: Same genus, consolidated SKU. Confirms stiff hair-like green growth on hard substrate, slow growth rate.

additional.name: AquariumDomain — Turtle Weed Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-reference for Chlorodesmis fastigiata. Provided temperature 72–81°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, high lighting, expert care level, propagation by fragmentation/sporulation, maximum 8 in height.

additional.name: Marine Plant Book — Turtle Weed
additional.notes: Second cross-reference. Confirmed high lighting, 72–80°F, advanced care level, moderate growth, 8 in max height, native range Australia/Eastern Caribbean/S. Pacific, depth 10–50 ft (~3–15 m), good nutrient uptake.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Chlorodesmis
additional.notes: Taxonomy source: family Udoteaceae, order Bryopsidales, class Ulvophyceae, division Chlorophyta. Also documented chlorodesmin toxin and coral interaction.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
